Details:
Iodine and bromine were found to be very effective for inactivating ricin in animal injection studies. One study used high dosages and extended contact times (e.g., 300 mg/L for a contact time of 1,200 minutes). However, another study showed that iodine was not effective in oxidizing ricin at a lower dosage of 16 mg/L.  These data suggest bomine and iodine may be effective in drinking water treatment.
Ricin inactivation in water by 4-diazophenyl-B-d-galactopyranoside was found to be relatively effective (50% to 90% inactivation at a ratio of 25 and 125 mol of 4-diazophenyl-B-d-galactopyranoside to ricin, respectively, with a contact time of 90 minutes).  Acetic acid and urea were found to effectively inactivate ricin in high dosages for one study; up to 90% inactivation with acetic acid and >97% inactivation with urea were observed.  pH was not given.  These data are only suggestive as they are not representative of drinking water treatment conditions.
